Thanks for the question. My rationale is explained as below:
1. XLA is independent of Tensorflow, although they are in the same repo now. There are talks about separating XLA from Tensorflow although I don't know when that will happen. In addition, XLA (as a DL compiler) is used by frontends other than Tensorflow, such as JAX and Pytorch (not in its main repo, I guess), etc.
2. I certainly can understand that it is inaccurate to say XLA is a framework. However, as XLA is independent of Tensorflow, it does not use Tensorflow constructs and _cannot_ use [TFOpContext](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/0a421945667ab0d914db588bcd48134f48dd0f05/horovod/tensorflow/mpi_ops.cc#L171). It then needs to create a new [XLAOpContext ](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/2337d8180a0c52076ac4224c7a35031fa2af726e/horovod/tensorflow/xla_mpi_ops.cc#L441) with a new "framework" name. I don't see alternatives.
3. If the name is really confusing to people, I may suggest rename "framework" to "backend".

For this bug fix one, yes it technically is. Specifically the `config_store` is a public interface via the `Session.get_component()` API. So a user could deepcopy the original `config_store` on the session, update it, and reattach it to the session. The bug is that the deep copy would not have the same default overrides that were provided to the original config store set here: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/a587e9261d11473e10491a1b09a55a0148951334/botocore/session.py#L255




For the `enhancement` one, we don't believe it will impact end users. When creating a client, the `config_store` is only used at instantiation of the client so providing a copy of it instead to the client should not impact an end to end user (i.e. it is not possible to update the `config_store` on the session and expected the change to permeate to a preexisting client).

The purpose of adding a changelog entry for it was more for awareness/acknowledge that we did make this subtle change if you are more knowledgeable on the architecture of botocore. It was labeled as an enhancement because there are not really any other categories it fits into. The argument for calling it an enhancement was that the client's config store is now more inline with the client's event emitters where they are snapshot copies of the original config_store/event_emitter from the session. I can go either way on a changelog for this or not.

Actually, this is a trick for Python 2 to support changing the value of outer scope variable. In Python 3 there is a keyword `nonlocal` which declares that the variable is to be found in the outer scope and the inner functions may actually set this reference. There is no such thing in Python 2. There is a kw `global` but it specifies that the variable is to be found in the global (module-level) scope. But the trick used here is that, although you can't set the variable from the outer scope, you can access/read them. Thus, the reference to the list `has_change` is read inside of the inner function and only the content of the list has been changed.

When we drop support for Python 2 one day, this can be changed to use `nonlocal`.

> So if the given block is too old you want to skip the preconditions checks the second time and go ahead and send the transaction anyway

No. Instead of having two calls to `register_secret_batch` I would have only one, no `try-except` here. The behavior of the `register_secret_batch` would be:

- Check if for the given block number the state can be queried, if it cannot then we skip the preconditions checks. (This only means we can't detect programming errors).
- Then do the `eth_estimateGas`  call to see if the transaction can be sent, the logic would proceed as it is, if the gas estimation fails, the transaction would fail, and therefore it's not sent.

The point here is: The `given_block_identifier` is the block identifier which generate the request, and it's sole purpose is to allows us to do precondition checking, and the sole purpose of the precondition checking is to detect programming errors. If we change the `given_block_number` to `latest`, then we are using the wrong generating block, and the precondition checks will have a false positive, e.g.:

Sending a secret register *after the secret has registered and the node has processed the given block* is a programming error, so consider this case:

- The secret is registered a block `n`
- The node emits a `ContractSendSecretRegister` at block `n + x`
- Because the node's block is larger then `n`, we know that the node should have processed that block and it must know the secret was registered, therefore we have a programming error.

Consider the following case:

- The node emits a `ContractSendSecretRegister` at block `n` and crashes
- The secret is registered by some other node at a block `n + x`
- The node restarts at block `n + x + y`, at this point, the precondition check should not fail, but the transaction should not be sent, because at the time the node tried to register the secret, that action was valid, but at the time the secret was being registered, it was not necessary anymore

Now, if `y` from above is large enough, say `128` block, and we change `n` to  `latest`, then what would be a perfectly fine call becomes a programming error. For this reason, I don't think we should change that value.

def fn2(x):

number = 10000

fn1_time = timeit.timeit(lambda: fn1(x), number=number)

Hi @bolkedebruin, can you elaborate on your objection? Once the code reaches a node of the DAG and finds outlets, it stops attempting to find further tasks upstream as the upstream tasks  are not placed into the stack. In the case, for example, that the task will have multiple (say, for e.g., 2) upstream tasks, then xcom_pull will be called twice compared to the previous code. However, since xcom_pull fires off a seperate database call (using get_one) for each task_id in the task_ids parameter, I don't think that this should have an appreciable difference in speed. 

Regarding using something like topological sort, I feel like that would lose too much information; in what I regard as the 'expected' behaviour, it requires discrimination between upstream or sibling  nodes, as you want to stop when outlets from a branch are found. As again, since that will do individual database queries, I'm not sure that it will be more efficient.

I could change it so that it doesn't do a database query on the task, but thats a relatively small change. 

Thanks for looking over the PR, and tell me if I have misunderstood something.

The reason why I detached it from the channel is that, from the user's point of view, it is easier to automatize all measurements if they are all in the same place. In conclusion, the reason for the current implementation is the ease of use from the client side.

@LongnoseRob, I think your idea of putting the common part in this file is great. If you don't mind, let me share few idea to increase code reuse. I think that status register is in some way pivotal in this instruments, as such I would like to propose the following:
- Add a `StatusBitsBase` class that represent generic status register and associated methods
- `StatusBitsBase` includes a mechanism to decode and encode bitfields
- In the instrument file, create a proper `Status` class derived from `StatusBitsBase`, by adding all the relevant fields and the functions to encode/decode.
-  Status information can be accessed as property, e.g. `inst.status.Format` (`inst `is an instance of an instrument)

See below some sketch implementation.

```
class StatusBitsBase(ctypes.BigEndianStructure):
    """
    A bitfield structure containing the assignments for the status decoding
    """
    _pack_ = 1
    _get_process_ = {}
    _set_process_ = {}

    @staticmethod
    def _convert_from_bcd(bcd):
        """Converts a bcd value to a decimal value

        :param value: The value to unpack from bcd
        :returns: The number in decimal form
        """
        place, decimal = 1, 0
        while bcd > 0:
            nibble = bcd & 0xF
            decimal += nibble * place
            bcd >>= 4
            place *= 10
        return decimal

    def __str__(self):
        """
        Returns a pretty formatted string showing the status of the instrument

        """
        ret_str = ""
        for field in self._fields_:
            ret_str = ret_str + f"{field[0]}: {hex(getattr(self, field[0]))}\n"

        return ret_str

    def __getattribute__(self, name):
        val = super().__getattribute__(name)
        if name == "fields":
            return val
        if name in self.fields():
            process = super().__getattribute__('_get_process_')
            if name in process:
                val = process[name](val)

        return val

    def __setattr__(self, name, value):
        val = super().__getattribute__(name)
        if name in self.fields():
            process = super().__getattribute__('_set_process_')
            if name in process:
                value = process[name](value)

        super().__setattr__(name, value)

    def fields(self):
        return [desc[0] for desc in super().__getattribute__('_fields_')]

class HPLegacyInstrument(Instrument):
    """
    Class for legacy HP instruments from the era before SPCI, based on `pymeasure.Instrument`

    """

    status_desc = StatusBitsBase # To be overriden by subclasses

    def __init__(self, adapter, name, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(
            adapter, name,
            includeSCPI=False,
            **kwargs,
        )

        self.status_bytes_count = ctypes.sizeof(self.status_desc)
        log.info(f"Initializing {self.name}")

.
.
.
    @property
    def status(self):
        """
        Returns an object representing the current status of the unit.

        """
        
        self.write("B")
        reply = self.adapter.read_bytes(self.status_bytes_count)
        return self.status_desc.from_buffer(reply)

```

To provide some context as to the specific use-case:

```python3
class RandomHorizontalFlip(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, p: float = 0.5) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.p = p

    def forward(
        self,
        images: Tuple[Tensor, Tensor],
        disparities: Tuple[Tensor, Tensor],
        masks: Tuple[Tensor, Tensor],
    ) -> Tuple[Tuple, Tuple, Tuple]:

        img_left, img_right = images
        dsp_left, dsp_right = disparities
        mask_left, mask_right = masks

        if dsp_right is not None and torch.rand(1) > self.p:
            # horizontal flip at pixel level
            img_left, img_right = F.hflip(img_left), F.hflip(img_right)
            dsp_left, dsp_right = F.hflip(dsp_left), F.hflip(dsp_right)
            # mask flipping flow is heavily skewed towards how we handle
            # generating pseudo-masks via the transform compose
            if mask_left is not None and mask_right is not None:
                mask_left, mask_right = F.hflip(mask_left), F.hflip(mask_right)
            # flip the two stereo channels
            return (
                (mask_right, mask_left),
                (dsp_right, dsp_left),
                (mask_right, mask_left)
            )

        return images, disparities, masks
```

The transform requires some inputs that for some datasets might not exist, namely the right disparity map. This is due to the fact, that we perform a horizontal pixel flip, the new target is not the transformed version of the original target but the opposite channel flipped target. Therefor the transform needs to have access to all the information.

Were it not for this transform, we could have just abided by the previous flow template.

Let us not add the `,omitempty` encoding specifier here. The rule of thumb is "if the type is a pointer to something, pass `omitempty`, otherwise do not add it.

If you are unfamiliar with what "omitempty" does, there are a few (poorly written) blog posts that can give you an idea: https://vsupalov.com/go-json-omitempty/.

But the reason it is important to not include it here is that it makes using the `apitype` from the frontend more difficult.

If for whatever reason the `CurrentUpdateAuthor` field weren't set, and the default value of `""` were serialized to JSON, the field would be missing entirely. This means that any JavaScript code consuming the JSON type would need to check for `undefined` instead of just receiving the `""` value.

For our REST API type definitions, we want to err on the side of being as explicit as possible -- i.e. sending empty strings and 0s across the wire.

The exception however is when dealing with Go values that could be `nil`. (i.e. anything that is a pointer, `map`, etc.) In those cases, we sometimes do specify the `omitempty`. The rational is that the JavaScript client is already aware that the field may not be sent, and therefore isn't any harder to use. And omitting the field from the JSON output saves XX bytes, which slowly adds up.

e.g. when you added a field to `StackSummary`, using `omitempty` here is good:
`CurrentUpdate *CurrentUpdateStatus `json:"currentUpdate,omitempty"`, because the JSON result will just return `undefined` when looking for the key `"currentUpdate"`. However, if we have the JSON result of `CurrentUpdateStatus`, we want both `currentUpdateAuthor` and `currentUpdateStarted` to have a value. (Even if 0 or "".)

The original purpose of this trainer was to add a way to add `resources_per_trial` functionality to the Tuner API for functional parity with tune.run() ([see this doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1dbRvmSYRB4E0BZSETPoj4g33eq5P3kwfVVp5FFQsSuI/edit#heading=h.2z2oq296adj)). The three options we discussed were

1. Add as a parameter to RunConfig
2. Introduce `tune.with_resources()`
3. Introduce a function trainer

A general feedback was to use the ScalingConfig rather than a resource definition / placement group factory so users don't have to learn different concepts. To achieve this and still support requesting multiple bundles (as users can to with resources_per_trial), we keep the default scaling config including num_workers, but we just don't start these workers ourselves.

An alternative to this would be to introduce a different key to request additional bundles, or to stick with placement group factories. In that case, we should probably stick with 1 or 2, though.

```suggestion
[DQN](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~vmnih/docs/dqn.pdf) is a model-free off-policy RL algorithm and one of the first deep RL algorithms developed. DQN proposes using a neural network as a function approximator for the Q-function in Q-learning. The agent aims to minimize the L2 norm between the Q-value predictions and the Q-value targets, which is computed as 1-step TD. The paper proposes two important concepts, a target network and an experience replay buffer. The target network is a copy of the main Q network and is used to compute Q-value targets for loss-function calculations. To stabilize training, the target network lags slightly behind the main Q-network. Meanwhile, the experience replay stores all data encountered by the agent during training and is uniformly sampled from to generate gradient updates for the Q-value network.
```

:facepalm: My bad, sorry. [`resize_image_tensor`](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/e0467c64e337c0d1140a9f9a70a413b7268231f4/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L38-L54) is a better reference:

```py
@torch.jit.script
def pad_segmentation_mask(segmentation_mask: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    num_masks, old_height, old_width = segmentation_mask.shape[-3:]
    batch_shape = segmentation_mask.shape[:-3]
    segmentation_mask = segmentation_mask.view((-1, num_masks, old_height, old_width))

    # kernel
    new_height, new_width = old_height, old_width

    return segmentation_mask.view(batch_shape + (num_masks, new_height, new_width))
```

The else is taken when you do not specify an ID, as a default ID of -1 is specified in the python part (don't remember where, maybe in the generated `pytsk3.c`). This is the case when you specify `offset` and `length` only (for instance https://github.com/py4n6/pytsk/blob/886a4bab2979f780e26680b32aec8a50350fe2d2/examples/icat.py#L61)

If you do not care about ID, you can set 0 (or whatever ID, as it's not checked at all in this case) and set the `TSK_FS_FILE_READ_FLAG_NOID` flag (https://github.com/sleuthkit/sleuthkit/blob/9e4bc4b5c0d34dcb266191558337be1e199af3da/tsk/fs/tsk_fs.h#L696)

In `tsk_fs_file_read_type` (https://github.com/sleuthkit/sleuthkit/blob/9e4bc4b5c0d34dcb266191558337be1e199af3da/tsk/fs/fs_file.c#L474), the `fs_attr` value is retrieved using the `type` (and `id` if the `TSK_FS_FILE_READ_FLAG_NOID` flag is not specified)
https://github.com/sleuthkit/sleuthkit/blob/9e4bc4b5c0d34dcb266191558337be1e199af3da/tsk/fs/fs_file.c#L499  
Adn then the content of the attribute is read with `tsk_fs_attr_read`.

This is also what happens in `tsk_fs_file_read` where the default attribute is retrieved with `tsk_fs_file_attr_get` and then also read with `tsk_fs_attr_read`


To sum things up,  if we didn't need the `id` parameter, we should set the `TSK_FS_FILE_READ_FLAG_NOID` flag to retrieve the content of a an attribute (specified by `type`) without caring about its ID.
But as we could need to retrieve an `id` with value 0, this change is needed and it should be the caller responsibility to set the `TSK_FS_FILE_READ_FLAG_NOID` flag if he wants data from a specific `type` but without specifying the ID. (as is done in libtsk actually)

I don't use this library, so I was making some assumptions about how it is used in the wild when I first implemented this feature. My original design goals were to be the least intrusive possible and backwards-compatible with existing installations, where the `client_secret` is stored in plaintext. That's why `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` was made optional.

So that's what sparked the pattern checking as an indicator of whether a `client_secret` was hashed or not. I had 2 use cases in mind:

* a new installation where there is no existing oauth data stored
* an existing installation where there is existing oauth data stored

For the first case, just set `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` and all client secrets are hashed correctly from the start.

For the second case, if you set `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` then you immediately have the possibility of a mixed data set, some secrets hashed and some not. And, if you ever change the hashing scheme in that config, you have the possibility of an even more mixed set with multiple schemes.

It was this second case I had in mind with the character checks, since relying solely on `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` to determine whether to compare with `check_password` rather than simple `!=` meant that existing secrets would no longer work.

I considered the approach you suggest, with a required migration of all existing secrets to use the hashing scheme. That just felt more intrusive to me. But again, I don't use the library, so it wasn't clear to me what was considered "normal" and what was not.

It seems highly unlikely that anyone would be manually setting application client secrets, since they are by default randomly generated strings with a constrained character set, and it seems even more unlikely that someone would deliberately use three `$` characters. So that was not a use case I considered. It's true that it's a security risk, but such a small one (after all, if a bad actor can manually set client secrets, your system has bigger problems) that I didn't consider it worth spending time on.

If you believe it's appropriate to add a migration to change all existing client secrets to use hashing, rather than checking string patterns at run time, I can do that. That's what I would call the "all or nothing" approach. There are a couple risks:

* I believe we should also then make `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` required rather than optional, so that we know what scheme to use, and relatedly,
* If someone were to set it, or change it, after the migration has run, then they would need to manually migrate all their secrets again

In other words, crossing the border from plaintext to hashed via a config setting means that individual installations take on the burden of maintaining their own data sets, in a way similar to passwords, with custom migrations, etc. That feels way outside the scope of this library, just as it is outside the scope of Django's password feature. If you want to change the scheme for your installation, you also assume the burden of migrating data.

If `oauth2_settings.CLIENT_SECRET_HASHER` were not only required but also hard coded to a particular scheme like `pbkdf2_sha256` as we do in the tests (i.e. not configurable in settings but a constant defined somewhere), then I think it's fair that this library also maintain its own migrations.

As I said at the top, one of my design biases was to be the least intrusive possible, so creating a new optional config was more in line with my bias than forcing a setting and adding a migration (and assuming the burden of that setting and subsequent migrations in the future).

Thanks for reading. Interested to hear what you think.

It does include a port if the netrc entry happens to have a port. If it doesn't have a port (a traditional netrc entry), urllib actually creates a second entry that does include the port (80 or 443 as appropriate).

```
$ python2.7
Python 2.7.18 (default, Oct 10 2021, 22:29:32) 
[GCC 11.1.0] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from urllib2 import HTTPPasswordMgrWithDefaultRealm
>>> manager = HTTPPasswordMgrWithDefaultRealm()
>>> manager.add_password(realm=None, uri="http://foo:9999", user="foo", passwd="bar")
>>> manager.add_password(realm=None, uri="http://bar", user="bar", passwd="baz")
>>> manager.passwd
{None: {(('foo:9999', '/'),): ('foo', 'bar'), (('bar', '/'),): ('bar', 'baz'), (('bar:80', '/'),): ('bar', 'baz')}}
```

IOW it's the opposite to what you suggest, urllib compares against the host name (possibly containing port) - it does not strip port.

Kazoo intentionally eschews monkey patching, see the documentation below. It expects you to explicitly choose the handler yourself if you use gevent.

https://kazoo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/async_usage.html#connection-handling
```
Kazoo doesn’t rely on gevents/eventlet monkey patching and requires that you pass in the appropriate handler, the default handler is SequentialThreadingHandler
```

Kazoo docs have pretty strong guidance about not using `SequentialThreadingHandler` with gevent.

https://kazoo.readthedocs.io/en/2.6.1/api/handlers/threading.html?highlight=sequentialthreadinghandler#kazoo-sasl-handlers-threading
```
Do not use SequentialThreadingHandler with applications using asynchronous event loops (like gevent). Use the SequentialGeventHandler instead.
```

My understanding from reading the [code](https://kazoo.readthedocs.io/en/2.6.1/_modules/kazoo_sasl/handlers/threading.html#SequentialThreadingHandler) and [GitHub issues](https://github.com/python-zk/kazoo/issues/3#issuecomment-7102481) is that the Zookeeper C client library is not patched by gevent and so [spawns a separate thread](https://gevent.narkive.com/Vn0vZP9R/is-it-possible-to-execute-code-in-main-thread-when-called-from-another-thread). However, this is not well documented and we haven't been _knowingly_ hurt by it.

`SequentialThreadingHandler` _did_ seem to work okay before we patched gevent queues in #659, where we found that the handler used queue methods not available on gevent queues. This brought `SequentialGeventHandler` to our attention, which was designed to work with gevent queues from the start.

I think using an implementation built for explicitly for gevent rather than a monkey patched one is a good thing. Not only will we avoid issues with the C lib that we don't even know about, gevent code can have different semantics and performance characteristics.

There are no `magic version values`. There is the concept of a default version for the purposes of having an initial version. Previously the gapic generator was using `0.1.0` as the default or initial version. This initial version was actually incorrect and should have been `0.0.0`. Release-please doesn't consider this to be a `magic version`, rather an initial version.

Release-please will use the version in the `.release-please-manifest.json` file to determine the next version of a library. It will update the version in `gapic_version.py` with whatever version it deems to be the next one based on the version in `.release-please-manifest.json`.

> When the update to the .py files is pushed, that will trigger this action, which will "update" the manifest and config file, except that "update" will be redundant at that point. Is that correct?

I wouldn't mention Zookeeper or any other DCS at all here.

TL;DR - there are two levels of caches:
1. `self._cluster` which is accessed via `cluster` property from REST API and cached only for `ttl` seconds.
2. sometimes DCS are have their own caches:
  - zookeeper does it for the entire Cluster
  - K8s and Etcd3 have caches for all keys associated with the cluster

The second type of caches exists to avoid reading something from DCS on every heart beat (when it is possible), and we shouldn't reveal here any implementation details.

So it is superfluous at face value, but it also gives a lot of intrinsic information to any LEM Log and event manager that a consumer may be using to help that log and event manager automatically extract context from the log/runtime. The `<field>="value"` allows most LEMs to both recognize the data and tag it.

For example:
 -  in ELK you can use a [plugin filter](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/logstash/current/plugins-filters-kv.html) to auto extract key words from the text 
- in Splunk you the [default key value](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/Splunk/8.0.2/Knowledge/Automatickey-valuefieldextractionsatsearch-time) mode is as described `key=value` 

The fields added here, error code: for reference in the library , the systemMessage: from the runtime, and the message: a human readable dictation of the raised error/event - all contribute value adds in a separate context to help enrich or tell that story to both the consumer at REPL then again at a LEM.

Good point, I didn't consider this before. PyCLIF doesn't have function overloading, it does not matter there.

Adding the callable check is easy (done), but I don't know if there is a way to check if the callable can be called without arguments (without actually calling it).

The conditions under which the missing check for number of arguments could go wrong are very special:

* obj needs to have `__getitem__` (to pass `PyMapping_Check()`)
* obj needs to have `items` and `items` must be callable
* there must be at least two overloads for the function for which we're converting from Python to C++
* one of the overloads must expect `std::map` (or similar)
* another the wrapped C++ type in the same position
* the overloads must be ordered so that the `std::map` conversion is tried first

Only then will the desired overload will not be reached.

Even if we can check for the number of arguments, `.items()` could have some arbitrary return value and we'd fall over processing that.

The only truly certain alternative is to not have `PyMapping_Check()` at all.

From memory: not having the `PyMapping_Check()` + `.items()` path would not be easy to accommodate. I think I'd have to make pretty intrusive changes to user code.

On the other hand, having the requirement that an object with `.__getitem__()` & `.items()` must implement these to be compatible with a Python mapping seems very natural (what most everybody would expect and not easily violate). What do you think?

But in any event, adding the callable check is easy and I'm happy to keep that.

Yes, and this bug is hard to find. I discussed this behavior in the following context:

> Backend: Synced with @rickyyx, we removed the optimization that distributed some filters in the GCS layer to decrease the latency of filtering.

Previously, we had an optimization for filters where we distributed some filters in the GCS layer to decrease the latency of filtering. For example, if we have two filters, task_id, and state:

Previously:
`GCS -> stateAPI (applied 2 filter conditions) -> API router
`

After the optimization:
`GCS (1 filter applied) -> stateAPI (1 filter applied) -> API router
`

Applying a filter at each layer is faster than applying all filters at the stateAPI layer. However, this will make the API `get_all_task_info` not pure since it modifies the data after searching. Therefore, we removed the filter optimization here.

Instead of having this concept of deployment groups and batching things like this, we could do 

```python
for _ in range(min(workers_needed, SIZE)):
``` 

and then if we don't have enough workers we could 

```python
if workers_needed > SIZE:
    raise kopf.TemporaryError(
        "Add maximum number of workers for this batch but still need to create more workers, " 
        f"waiting for {DELAY} seconds before continuing.", 
        delay=DELAY)
```

This will cause `kopf` to stop executing `daskworkergroup_replica_update`, wait for the delay period and then call it again.

https://kopf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/errors/#temporary-errors

I think this would simplify things a lot here. We just let `kopf` keep calling `daskworkergroup_replica_update` until we have all the workers we requested. It also means if the autoscaler reduces the number of workers in the meantime we will stop scaling up.

> The Segmentation models rescale and normalize the images outside of the models meaning you would have to undo them before vizualizing.

Users **have to** convert their images to float in [0, 1] at some point.

> All pre-trained models expect input images normalized in the same way,
i.e. mini-batches of 3-channel RGB images of shape (3 x H x W),
where H and W are expected to be at least 224.
The images have to be loaded in to a range of [0, 1] and then normalized
using ``mean = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]`` and ``std = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]``.

So it's not just something contained in the transforms. It's pervasive throughout the library, and throughout the entire ecosystem. By supporting those images directly, we allow users to drop the uint8 images and only keep the float images (normalized and non-normalized versions).

According to https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/3774#discussion_r629332764, Mask-RCNN and Faster-RCNN don't require normalization, so one could use the same non-normalized float images in this case I think.

IIUC, the main concern seems to be the assumption that if an image is a float, then we expect it to be in [0, 1]. [According to our docs](https://pytorch.org/vision/master/transforms.html), this assumption holds for the transforms already: 

> The expected range of the values of a tensor image is implicitely defined by the tensor dtype. Tensor images with a float dtype are expected to have values in [0, 1). Tensor images with an integer dtype are expected to have values in [0, MAX_DTYPE] where MAX_DTYPE is the largest value that can be represented in that dtype.

It also holds when calling `F.convert_image_dtype(img, torch.float)` and all our conversion utilities as far as I can tell, so it seems to be a reasonable assumption.

I'll add a comment in the docstring to clarify that. The worst thing that can happen if the user passes a float image that's not in [0, 1] is that the colors will be slightly off

>I generally think these patterns where are an is constructed and then is setup/initialized with a non-ctor method is stateful and error-prone.

I agree and have been mentally turning this over looking for a better solution.

>Can we make this instead an abstract class method that ends up return an instance of class?

So the fundamental issue here is that we have two sets of params (1) user-provided params on the ext side, which might be anything for a custom loader/writer. (2) params passed over the orch side which need to be extracted from the environment. Both sets of params need to be combined in the same loader or writer object.

The difficulty is that they come from different sources and are available at different times. (1) are specified by the user in the external script. Since (2) are passed by the orchestrator, they are available only at context initialization time.

I see 3 ways to handle this scenario:

- **A (status quo)**: User passes an instance to `init_dagster_externals`. Instance has been constructed with params (1). The instance itself is responsible for extracting (2) from the environment and modifying itself accordingly.
- **B (this PR)**: User passes an instance to `init_dagster_externals`. Instance has been constructed with params (1). `init_dagster_externals` handles extraction of (2) from env and calls `setup` with extracted (2) to mutate the object.
- **C**: Same as A except instead of mutating the object, `init_dagster_externals` copies it and applies params (2) (perhaps the constructor accepts them).
- **D**: User passes some intermediate entity containing the class and params (1) that is not an instance. When params (2) are available they are combined with params (1) and passed together to the constructor.

I like (D), which lambdas fall under. What do you think of either of these:

```
### "lambda solution"
init_dagster_externals(
    context_loader=lambda params: CustomContextLoader(foo=foo, bar=bar, **params)
)

### "partials solution"
init_dagster_externals(
    # `partial` will be defined on base classes and return a simple object having
    # the `setup` method that returns `CustomContextLoader`
    context_loader=CustomContextLoader.partial(foo=foo, bar=bar)
)
```

If we want this to be pluggable in an intuitive way, IMO users should be able to pass an instance of a `ContextLoader`/`MessageWriter` to `init_dagster_externals`. This gives them full control over the "first round of parameter binding" (i.e. they can structure the constructor and its args however they'd like). And if they pass an instance, then the second round (params passed over from orch side)

`GetAllNodeInfo` returns all nodes info including dead nodes. We needs to know how to merge them to ip. For example, there are two nodes info:
- {node id: 1, hostname: example}
- {node id: 2, hostname: example}

We choose which one for host example?

For one hostname, there will be a list of node info. and only two cases:
- All nodes info of one hostname are DEAD
- Only one node info of one hostname is ALIVE

So, here is the rule,
- Choose a DEAD one if all nodes info of one hostname are DEAD. 
- Choose the ALIVE one if there is ALIVE node in one hostname.

No, batch systems that don't use the feature ought to return an empty list.

Batch systems that do use the feature will return the contexts that they need the worker to run jobs in. The leader queries the batch system and passes the results along to the worker. If a batch system doesn't override the method, the leader needs to get a result that says that no contexts are needed (i.e. an empty list).

You don't pass the contexts *into* the batch system for it to hold and give back. The batch system is responsible for deciding what contexts are needed.

> Hi @ashione, here are more context. If we define `ReportMetrics(MetricPoints, OpenCensusData)`. The issue here are two folds:
> 
> * MetricPoints are derived from OpenCensusData. This means if we only need OpenCensus data, the MetricsPoints computation is redundant. (For the MetricExporterClient implementations, the `ReportMetrics` are implemented in Either-Or.)
> * `MetricPoints` has different length than `OpenCensusData` because the distribution values from `OpenCensusData` are expanded into max, min, and average. This makes it difficult for batching.
> 
> Additionally, the MetricExporterClient makes it possible to just override one method: `ReportMetrics(MetricPoints)` and leave the OpenCensus on unimplemented. This marks it clear the `MetricPoints` are the only implementation required for all sublcasses and use the decorator chain to pass on the data. `ReportMetrics(OpenCensusData)` is entirely optionally and fall back to empty implementation block (Noop with a warning). `ReportMetrics(OpenCensusData)` will not go through the decorator chain.

Hmm that's not how I read our discussion. Pasting the pseudocode snippet here:

```python
nonblockchain_preconditions()
blockchain_preconditions(consistent_block)  # this will also function as a check of the logic that lead us to start the call in the first place
gas = gas_estimate(pending_block)
if gas is not None:
  txhash = send_transaction()
  result = poll(txhash)
if gas is None or result is Failure:
    transaction_mined = gas is not None
    known_race, msg = check_blockchain_conditions(mined_block if transaction_mined else pending_block)
    if known_race:
        raise RaidenRecoverableError('{} {}'.format('Transaction Failed' if transaction_mined else 'Transaction will fail', msg))
    else:
        raise RaidenUnrecoverableError("Sad face")  
```

You can see that there are blockchain and other precondition checks before estimate gas. And then blockchain checks again after failure (either estimate or transaction send failure).

> Edit: If we do decide to do the checks after the estimate gas, then we should do all of them, and then it would not make sense to have it before and after estimate gas)

This is similar to what I had proposed before in our conversation [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/2988#discussion_r246909831) where I proposed to start with `estimateGas` and only if that fails check conditions.

To that you asked:

> Why would you want to do the estimate gas before the preconditions checks? What does this achieve?

from which we went to the approach we agreed on.

---

But looking at the current version of `proxies/token_network.sol` it seems that `new_netting_channel()` is the only call in proxies which does not follow the pattern we agreed on above, so it definitely needs adjustment. The second (postconditions) checks should be under a combined estimateGas/transaction sent failure. Not duplicated in both cases.

There's two parts to answer your question.

If the topic is not included, the server returns a 400 with error message `"Invalid Google Cloud Pub/Sub topic. It should look like '//pubsub.googleapis.com/projects/*/topics/*.'"`

In our client, on [line 270](https://github.com/googleapis/python-storage/blob/b32d2ccabef21fa3dbdfba34acde85618a785d48/google/cloud/storage/notification.py#L270), we preprocess the request body `topic` through string interpolation. So topic becomes `"//pubsub.googleapis.com/projects/<project-identifier>/topics/None"` if  `topic_name=None`. Thus we get a 400 for another reason `"Cloud Pub/Sub topic '//pubsub.googleapis.com/projects/project/topics/None' not found, ..."`

To avoid overdoing client-side validation, I could instead change the logic around preparing/formatting the topic.

No, as I said, the original logic was incredibly flawed as it was manipulating the lists while iterating over them, and the index based removals were costly performance wise, making it O(M*N^2) (each iteration, we might remove element at arbitrary index, which is O(N)), that all was happening M times (until motd stops changing). 

The new approach is O(M*N), we repeat M times, and the individual processes each just take O(N), the final removal then takes another O(N), but O(N) + O(N) + O(N) + ... is still just O(N), not O(N^2), making the new approach still win over the previous one in performance. On top of that, it doesn't introduce any hacky removals during iteration, which is an anti-pattern which should be avoided in pretty much ALL cases.

Note: previously I had referenced "Do One Thing," a concept Uncle Bob uses when talking about functions, when I actually meant to reference "Single Responsibility Principle," which he uses when talking about classes. Here's a relevant quote from the chapter on Classes near where he's talking about the Single Responsibility Principle:

> The name of a class should describe what responsibilities it fulfills. In fact, naming is probably the first way of helping determine class size. If we cannot derive a concise name for a class, then it’s likely too large. The more ambiguous the class name, the more likely it has too many responsibilities. For example, class names including weasel words like `Processor` or `Manager` or `Super` often hint at unfortunate aggregation of responsibilities. We should also be able to write a brief description of the class in about 25 words, without using the words “if,” “and,” “or,” or “but.”

```python
def normalize(lat, lng):
    """ Helper to coerce lat/lng range
    
    lat in [-90, +90]
    lng in [-180, +180]
    
    """
    while lat > +90:
        lat -= 180
    
    while lat < -90:
        lat += 180

    while lng > +180:
        lng -= 360
        
    while lng < -180:
        lng += 360

    return lat, lng
```

Furthermore, the main idea to append the `httpie` into Dockerfile is the nature of *Kinto* to communicate over http(s) protocol. So, it seems attaching `httpie` to the container helps developers to be more equipped in order to test the application omnidirectionally.
Besides, it actually doesn't matter to be installed by either of `pip3` and `apt-get` because it's a handy tool to make get/post/put/delete/patch requests more easily. As I mentioned, if the purpose of adding `httpie` has to cover all aspects, that is, test stuff throughout the command line and pure python scripts, yeah, both `apt-get` and `pip3` should have been involved unless it would be enough to choose one amongst

> pip3 install pip --upgrade && apt-get httpie

where the latter preferably will be installed based on `Python2` core if it's already installed. If `pip3` after `apt-get` installs the `httpie` package and `httpie` has been installed and attached to `PATH` it does not just override the executable file but also install the eggs and it's ready to be imported in python program. Also, if you choose to install it by means of `pip3` it should be done in the following fashion why the upgrade has to be completed before the `pip3` starts to install `httpie`

> pip3 install pip --upgrade
> pip3 install httpie

Any `Python` interpreter has its own specific version of `pip` whatever the major and minor version of all `Python` interpreters are the same so that `pip` needs to be upgraded to the last stable version otherwise causes reporting to warn you to upgrade the `pip`; this was the why I offered you to append both segments including `pip3 install --upgrade pip` and `pip3 install httpie`, however, according to discussion `httpie` installation has no unique way.

Ah that's an interesting note about combining the conditionals.  There are approaches we can take to improve that without having to impact the formatting.

If we did need to keep a specific format, there are `# fmt: on` and `# fmt: off` directives we can use: https://github.com/psf/black#the-black-code-style

There are also `pylint: disable=line-too-long` for pylint and `# noqa: E501` for flake8 to disable length checks.

That said, it's usually best if we could do this while still staying within the limits.  One way is that Python does allow the conditionals to run over multiple lines, for example, instead of:

```
if foo and bar and baz:
```

We can write:
```
if (foo
    and bar
    and baz
):
```

and Python's parser will allow it because we've put the conditional expression inside `(parentheses)` which allows the expression to extend over multiple lines.

We could even do:

```
if foo \
    and bar \
    and baz:
```

but we try to avoid backslashes because they're visually noisy.  Another reason we avoid backslashes is because if someone accidentally adds a trailing space (which is pretty hard to spot, since it's a space) after the backslash it breaks the expression.  Thus, parentheses are usually the go-to for this kind of situation.

I'd be curious to see if breaking it up into multiple lines affects performance.  I wouldn't expect it to, but it is Python so I wouldn't be surprised either.

@wholmgren Unfortunately, this does not work, even though it is indeed much cleaner and understandable. When the request.get function formats the URL, it converts any equal signs in the parameter values to %3D and semi-colons to %3B. I found this by looking at the generated URL (res.url).

E.g. the first part of the data input section of the URL needs to be "&DataInputs=latitude=2.9;longitude=-54.0;altitude=-999;", but it is instead converted by the request.get function into "&DataInputs=latitude%3D2.9%3Blongitude%3D-54.0%3Baltitude%3D-999%3B" which is not recognized by the API.

However, adding the datainputs manually to the base URL and letting request.get format part of it works:

    base_url = f"http://{server}/service/wps"

    params = {

    res = requests.get(base_url + '?DataInputs=' + data_inputs, params=params)

>How would this affect downlevel SDKs that attempt to serialize new outputs?

They *may* get an exception at an earlier place than before.  i.e. at a point when some code awaits the 'isKnown' bit rather than the code awaiting the 'value' of the Output.

However, to be clear, that exception isn't something i believe ever actually happens in the wild.  Specifically, this code was written to be very paranoid about leaving Outputs in a bad state (i.e. with promises that never resolved, which could lead to hangs in apps).  The previous code simply made us be in the stated `(isUnknown: false, value: rejected)` the new code puts us in the state `(isKnown: rejected, value: rejected)`.  As any update would still have to get the value in either event, you would always end up throwing no matter what.

```python
# bip32:
# checksum_len = initial_entropy_len // 32
# total_len = initial_entropy_len + checksum_len
# hence total_len == checksum_len * 33
total_len = len(b)
checksum_len = l // 33
entropy_len = l - checksum_len
entropy_bits = b[: -checksum_len]
checksum_bits = b[-checksum_len:]
entropy = int(entropy_bits, 2).to_bytes(entropy_len // 8, "big")
checksum = int(checksum_bits, 2).to_bytes(checksum_len // 8, "big")
return hashlib.sha256(entropy).digest() == checksum
```

The run_sql() is the version that wait until the result is done. 

Here, the submit_sql() is the version that waits until the job_id is available (which we consider as an asynchronous version). However, the job_id may not be available immediately if the capacity is reached. That's why the blocking mode comes in, i.e. keep sending the job until the job_id is returned. Even though max_tries can serve that purpose, its default value doesn't guarantee the job is launched (not completed) successfully. In my original implementation, 'blocking=True' means an infinite loop of pooling the job queue until it is submitted successfully. However, the use of the backoff package prevents me from doing this (at least I could not find a way to impose infinite loop), and I set to the very high values. Also, the interval between tries is not infinite, as I also impose a delay (3seconds) between tries.

So my general rule would be to avoid `kwargs` by default since it hides the exact arguments (ie. "explicit is better than implicit") and tends to make debugging more difficult accordingly. That's a lot more on the method arguments side of things than in calling code, mind -- ie. I appreciate that you have this method taking `session = None, timeout = None` as args instead of `**kwargs` and pulling them out from a readability perspective -- ie. this tells us that "this function cares about timeout values". That makes sense given how the `put()` method works; eg. see its method definition:
```python
@abstractmethod
async def put(self, url: str, headers: Dict[str, str], data: IO[Any],
              timeout: int) -> Response:
    pass
```
Since that function explicitly asks for a `timeout` argument, we should in turn explicitly pass it one. In that case, since we're in a different context, it makes sense to consider what our default should be here (which may just-so-happen to be a duplicate value!) and mark it as such. In other words:
```python
async def create_subscription(...,  timeout: int = 10) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    # SNIP..
    resp = await s.put(url, data=encoded, headers=headers, timeout=timeout)
```

### Why Is Duplicating This Value Ok?

So I think the whole "Don't Repeat Yourself" thing has taken on a muddled meaning over the years. "Don't Repeat Yourself" should not mean "literally do not have duplicate code anywhere". It instead should mean "do not duplicate _concepts_ which should be constant in all usecases".

_This is not one of those cases_! The session's `def put(.., timeout=10)` value is describing "what should the default timeout value be for _arbitrary put requests made to Google services_"? The subscriber's `def create_subscription(.., timeout=10)` value is describing "what should the default timeout value be for _the create subscription request in particular_"? The fact that those have identical values **is not duplication**.

### When Would We Use Kwargs?

Kwargs are super useful in cases where you want to signal that your method does not (**and should not**) care about a given argument. Most good examples along these lines have to do with inheritence-style composition: for example, consider the standard OO case of having an `Animal` class and a `Dog` subclass. You probably want the `Dog` subclass to be defined with methods along the lines of "do these  Dog-specific things... and then also whatever the more general Animal stuff is". For example:
```python
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name: str, species: str) -> None:
        # SNIP

class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs) -> None:
        super()._init__(self, **kwargs)
```

This lets you be more explicit about which things you are changing, ie. something along the lines of:
```python
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name: str, species: str) -> None:
        # SNIP

class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, has_dog_breath: bool, **kwargs) -> None:
        self._needs_a_toothbrush = has_dog_breath
        super()._init__(self, **kwargs)
```

In this specific example, using `kwargs` in the `create_subscription()` argument would be tying the implementation details together too closely -- it would be akin to saying "whatever extra stuff the put method does"... which would be pretty confusing and implicit to users, since they then need to know that we just-so-happen to be using `Session.put` later on in the method, which is an implementation detail our users should not have to care about!

Maple gave me a closed form solution for the Hankel transform -- a crazy combination of hypergeometric functions; see below for a -> ascii printout of the result. However, emprically, as I increased the accuracy of the calculations, I got answers that seemed to get quite close to 11/3 exactly.  Numbers like 3.66675 for instance. Are there any compositions which this featurizer won't work for, and which can be checked easily?

E.g., if there is a composition with 15 elements, will roost still work?

If so, RoostFeaturizer needs a `precheck` method. See the `BaseFeaturizer.precheck` method for more details (reproduced here):

```
"""
        Precheck (provide an estimate of whether a featurizer will work or not)
        for a single entry (e.g., a single composition). If the entry fails the
        precheck, it will most likely fail featurization; if it passes, it is
        likely (but not guaranteed) to featurize correctly.

        Prechecks should be:
            * accurate (but can be good estimates rather than ground truth)
            * fast to evaluate
            * unlikely to be obsolete via changes in the featurizer in the near
                future

        This method should be overridden by any featurizer requiring its
        use, as by default all entries will pass prechecking. Also, precheck
        is a good opportunity to throw warnings about long runtimes (e.g., doing
        nearest neighbors computations on a structure with many thousand sites).

        See the documentation for precheck_dataframe for more information.

        Args:
            *x (Composition, Structure, etc.): Input to-be-featurized. Can be
                a single input or multiple inputs.

        Returns:
            (bool): True, if passes the precheck. False, if fails.

```

Sorry, I worded this wrong and I should've been more precise. I meant the output type choice.

> The `_get_streams` method for the plugin has already been called

It's up to the plugin's `get_{author,category,title}` methods to return the metadata, which means there could be fetching logic in these methods instead of `_get_streams`, and fetching metadata when it's not necessary should be avoided to prevent unnecessary stream launch delays. Most plugins currently fetch metadata in `_get_streams` and return the cached data in these metadata getter methods, but that's not a guaranteed pattern.

What's important is the output type (passthrough, http, json, stream-url, player/file) that gets decided in `handle_stream`, which means it would only make sense reading metadata in `output_stream`, as this method is responsible for creating the output (player or file) where we need the substituted file name. The `output_stream` call is however part of a for-loop, which depends on its return value, which again depends on its inner `success_open` variable, and this must be `True`. Since you need the substituted file name in the `console.exit` and `progress` calls after the `create_output()` call, it probably makes more sense getting the metadata in `output_stream` rather than `create_output`, or you'd have to return a tuple in `create_output`.

Good point. Yeah, your code is actually technically incorrect as you aren't handling endianness. But it will only provide false negatives. The format strings do specify endianness. This is specified in https://docs.python.org/3/library/struct.html#byte-order-size-and-alignment

Note that if there is no endian specifier, the default is "native", so your code won't return any false positives.

Your current code will return false negatives. "@i" and "=i" for example are equivalent to "int", but your code will return false. "<i" will be equivalent to int on little-endian platforms. ">i" and "!i" on big-endian platforms. Etc, etc.

    def move(self, new_position: float, timeout: float = None):
        from bluesky.run_engine import call_in_bluesky_event_loop, in_bluesky_event_loop

> Why is this wrong?

This assumes that the item (`blob`) is a file, but it could have the executable bit (`100755`) or be a symlink (`120000`), or even a submodule (`160000`). More [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/69341082).

> Should we add a WI to correct it?

Probably not. This implementation needs these fixes (at least):

1.  The above file mode issue.
2.  It doesn't respect `.gitignore`. We'd need to implement this or add a [library](https://github.com/mherrmann/gitignore_parser/blob/master/gitignore_parser.py).
3.  It ignores any files or directories that start with a dot (`.forceignore`). Simple enough, but we'd need to add an option the consuming task. It'd need to continue ignoring them by default to avoid surprising users accidentally committing code.
4.  It doesn't handle submodules correctly.

None of these are showstoppers for the one task that uses the class, but are probably more impactful downstream in Metecho. Fixing all of these is doable, but I doubt the ROI is worth it, since we can just use Git (via subprocess or a library).

With the goal of `calcparams_*` methods' return values having the same Python type, I think we want the behavior of `np.isscalar` instead of `np.ndim(x) == 0`. If `calcparams_*` is given a mix of floats and 0d-arrays (e.g. `effective_irradiance` is a Python `float` and `temp_cell` is a 0d-array), using `np.ndim(x) == 0` will cause the `calcparams_*` methods' return values to be a mix of floats and 0d-arrays. If we use `np.isscalar`, all return values will become 0d-arrays. This is because `np.broadcast_arrays(1.0, np.array(2.0))` equals `[np.array(1.0), np.array(2.0)]`.

This is one place in the code where tabs were not handled correctly, yes. The original code worked for spaces but not for tabs just as you write.

And yes, there was some support for tab expansion but it didn't work according to the spec: "in contexts where spaces help to define block structure, tabs behave as if they were replaced by spaces with a tab stop of 4 characters." Tabs were always replaced with four spaces, when they should have been replaced with between 1 and 4 spaces depending on the position on the line. There is a tab stop every four positions on the line, and that's what the tabs should expand to.

The tab expansion added now is not perfect, because it doesn't take into account leading space that has been cut off from for example a list item. But it works as long as tabs are applied consistently, i.e., not mixing tabs and spaces too much. The "perfect" implementation is still possible but it's probably quite some work.

I see your point about how examples 312 and 313 are mentioned in the commit message to make it sound like they're related to tab expansion. They are *not*, but they *are* related to leading space in list items and  therefore they happened to get fixed by the same change. I'll try and make that a bit more clear.

I think that where we're not aligning here is that I'm taking a common-case API-centric view while I think that you're taking an `AggregateFn`-centric view. Users will mostly use this Pandas-like multi-aggregation API or the aggregation-specific APIs (`.mean()`), and will mostly not have to think about `AggregateFn`. The only time that they should have to use `AggregateFn` is if they're trying to do a multi-aggregation with a custom configuration of the aggregation (e.g. `Std(ddof=0)`), and for that case, I think that:
```python
ds.agg({
    "A": ["mean", Std(ddof=0), "min", "max"],
    "B": ["sum", "max"],
})
```
is much better UX than
```python
ds.agg([Mean("A"), Std("A", ddof=0), Min("A"), Max("A"), Sum("B"), Max("B")])
```

I'm also wanting to get the multi-aggregation API as close to the Pandas API as possible, which allows for aggregation functions in the column --> aggregations dict API.

>When people use them directly, they need to provide the on. Basically AggregateFn should be self contained.

I agree that the current use of `AggregateFn` is messy, but I think that's more of an issue with the `AggregateFn` abstraction design rather than its use. As I alluded to [here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/20090#discussion_r746149156), the lifetime of `on` doesn't line up with the rest of the `AggregateFn`, and is also conceptually distinct from the rest of `AggregateFn`, so it would be great if we could decouple these. Conceptually, I'd think of it as:

1. `AggregateFn` type: An implementation of the accumulator-based aggregation API.
2. `AggregateFn` instance: A configuration of an aggregation function.
3. `AggregateFn` instance + `on`: Specifies the data subset (e.g. columns) to apply the aggregation to.

`on` could technically be applied to the data before it's even given to the aggregation function (`agg_fn.accumulate()` in particular). Here's an abstraction design that I think might be better:
```python
class Aggregation:
    """A named aggregation on some subset of a dataset.
    """
    agg_fn: AggregateFn
    on_fn: Callable
    name: str

    def __init__(self, agg_fn: AggregateFn, on: Union[str, Callable], name: Optional[str] = None):
        self.agg_fn = agg_fn
        self.on_fn = _to_on_fn(on)
        if name is None:
            name = type(agg_fn).__name__.lower()
            if isinstance(on, str):
                name += "_{on}"
            else:
                name += "_{on.__name__}"
        self.name = name

class AggregateFn:
    init: Callable[[KeyType], AggType]
    accumulate: Callable[[AggType, T], AggType]
    merge: Callable[[AggType, AggType], AggType]
    finalize: Callable[[AggType], U] = lambda a: a

# Usage in `Block.combine()`

accumulators = [agg.agg_fn.init(next_key) for agg in aggs]
for r in gen():
    for i in range(len(aggs)):
        accumulators[i] = aggs[i].agg_fn.accumulate(
            accumulators[i], aggs[i].on_fn(r))
```

Decoupling the `name` and `AggregateFn` will also open the door for easily supporting [named aggregation](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/groupby.html#groupby-aggregate-named), which is a nice feature.

In fact, according to logging documentation, we should not do:

```python
logging.debug("Found %s version %s" % (project, release))
```
nor

```python
logging.debug("Found {} version {}".format(project, release))
```
But we should do:

```python
logging.debug("Found %s version %s", project, release)
```

The main difference here is the fact that logging is smart enough to do the formatting only when it's needed. With the two not recommended code, if the logging level is, say, info level, the debug message are not treated, but the formatting is still done.

FK's are handled natively by Django's core serializer. As for the other relationships...

Object serialization can get a little crazy. I think I'm trying to walk a fine line between storing the state of the object being changed but not attempting to store the state of the system. I recognize that this boundary can be murky when we consider M2M through tables and reverse FK relationships in general. 

I think if we wanted to expand the serialization beyond the scope of the immediate record, then we'd need to include an option to do something like the following. 

1. Examine meta properties of each of the objects fields.
2. For every reverse FK relationship or M2M field found:
    a. Query the related objects
    b. For each object found
        i. Serialize the result
        ii. Assemble a list of serialized dictionaries
    c. Bolt the list of dictionaries onto the field representation.

From my perspective, handling reverse FK relationships is not really worth it. Our team will simply log changes on the related objects separately so we can thread the data together later if necessary. I would be interested though if you thought this juice was really worth the squeeze (did I mention I like challenges). Alternatively, maybe you know about a more elegant approach for implementing nested serialization for reverse FK relationships?

> therefore it will not be skipped and will have state of either `success` or `failure`.

`failure` is not concrete enough. It will not be skipped in case of failure of the datasets tests. If the failure happens in any other step, e.g. `torchvision` installation, the step will be skipped.

> In this case, I think we dont need `steps.datasets.conclusion == 'failure'` conditions here since it is kinda redundant.

They are not redundant. If we leave them out, we are left with `if: success()` which is the default behavior if you don't define `if:` at all. Meaning, the step will only be run if *all* previous steps have succeeded. 

We need this step to run if *all setup steps* (everything before the first test step) have succeeded, regardless of the state of the datasets tests. IMO `success() || steps.datasets.conclusion == 'failure'` is shortest expression that gets us this behavior, but if you can find a better one, I'm all ears.

re: default.  Personally, I **much** prefer people to signal 'None' as a default parameter as the type can then be `Optional[str]` rather than `str` (which is a good signal that it is optional).  By setting the default if None is passed, it's really obvious in the code that the default is being used.  Finally, it stops (forever) the risk that someone will accidentally put an internally-mutable reference as a default leading to all sorts of shenanigans.

e.g.

```python
def some_function(arg=[]):
    arg.append("thing")
    print(arg)
```

:)

So, I'd like optional arguments to still be `None`.

A class inheriting ABC can only be instantiated if it implements every method the ABC decorates with `abstractmethod`. That's useful here because it enables InteractiveCredential.get_token to consolidate common code and do most of the work for subclasses while ensuring you get a runtime error when a subclass doesn't implement the one method that differs between authentication flows. With this change MsalCredential loses its only abstract method and thus its reason for inheriting ABC. InteractiveCredential still has an abstract method but was inheriting ABC through MsalCredential. So, now it must inherit ABC directly.

Here is my understanding of how the changes integrate with the implem:
- passing `py::preallocate()` as an extra argument of a new style constructor will preallocate and register just before actually calling the constructor of the class
- if the call is fine, the holder is still not registered making this piece of code to be called (only to init the holder then):
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/bbb89da152a0e4f63a4fecc0d4db44f0ad855fdf/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L1127-L1130
- if the call is not successful (because of cast error AFAIU), the next overload try (if any) will deallocate the memory if already allocated
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/bbb89da152a0e4f63a4fecc0d4db44f0ad855fdf/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L754-L758

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/4a035c44aa80a31be0af942db438b430e3ed3a57/pkg/codegen/dotnet/gen.go#L1116

While the C# compiler will help prevent you from passing `null` here with warnings, someone could force it, or a null value could come in from another language, so this is ensures it's initialized to an instance of the appropriate `Args` class. In fact it is not exactly this goal. The point is to make Certbot behaves correctly, and this imply most of the time to get consistent behavior across platforms. But not always, for instance the requirement to run Certbot as an administrator, while you can run Certbot as a normal user on Linux. 

It means that I do make converge behaviors across platforms not by principle, but by necessity.

I prefer to avoid converting this error into a `EEXIST` for the following reasons:
* Certbot works correctly without it
* Python developers did not convert this windows exception into a python one, which constitute a hint here in my opinion
* and in fact, the error is very generic: it is a `PermissionError`, not something like "a directory already exists at this path"

This makes me worry to accidentally shadow an unrelated exception into an EEXIST one, and create unexpected side effects in the future. Also as you said, it is not documented. It seems that no Windows developer thought it would be a good idea to document, and maybe handle specifically this case on a so much critical function as `CreatFile` is.

Instead you just get a "You are not authorized to do the action you are trying to do"...

Re https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/3680#issuecomment-821678875: The annotation files are structured like this:

```
label,youtube_id,time_start,time_end,split
clay pottery making,---0dWlqevI,19,29,train
...
```

It seems you are only using the fields `label` and `youtube_id`.

1. Is that correct? Shouldn't we respect `time_start` and `time_end`?
2. If we really only need two fields per row, we probably go the easy way:
    
    ```python
    import csv
    
    with open("annotations.csv") as fh:
        label, youtube_id = zip(*[row[:2] for row in csv.reader(fh, delimiter=",")])
    ```

    In case we also need access to the other fields, a [`DictReader`]() would probably a better choice. 

    In any case this will most likely be slower than the `pandas` implementation. We just recently removed the last `pandas` dependency in #3656. Before we reintroduce it here, we should profile this and see if it is a bottleneck.
